ut1-5-1.title: Hands-on: contact thickness survey on a step block
ut1-5-1.intro: Slide the 0° contact probe along the stepped block exactly as you would on a corrosion survey — only a thin couplant film separates the probe face from the steel. Flat, steady contact (not pressure) is what keeps the backwall echo repeatable while you gauge each step.

tasks.label: Record thickness readings on three different steps
tasks.hint: Keep the probe flat and still on each step — let the backwall echo settle before you record, just as a steady couplant film demands.

tasks.label: Identify the thinnest step on the block
tasks.hint: The thinnest step has the shortest round trip, so its backwall echo arrives earliest on the A-scan.

ut1-5-2.title: Hands-on: the 4:1 water-to-steel velocity rule
ut1-5-2.intro: Fire the same pulse through water and through steel and watch how much slower the couplant is — sound crosses water at roughly one quarter of its speed in steel. That 4:1 ratio is exactly why your immersion standoff must be at least thickness ÷ 4 (plus margin) before the water multiple clears the back-wall echo.

tasks.label: Send the pulse through water, then through steel
tasks.hint: Water carries sound at ~1,480 m/s vs steel's ~5,920 — that ~4× gap is the whole basis of the water-path ≥ thickness ÷ 4 rule.

tasks.label: Raise frequency until λ in steel is under 1 mm
tasks.hint: λ = v/f, so steel needs roughly 6 MHz or more — immersion's constant water coupling is what makes such high frequencies practical.

ut1-5-3.title: Hands-on: why air coupling runs below 1 MHz
ut1-5-3.intro: Drop the frequency to the slider's 1 MHz floor — already the ceiling of practical air-coupled work — and watch the wavelength stretch in water, aluminum, and steel. Velocity is the v in Z = ρv: air's ~330 m/s makes its impedance so low that R ≈ 1 at each interface, forcing air-coupled probes to low frequencies and coarser resolution.

tasks.label: Run the pulse through water, aluminum, and steel
tasks.hint: Compare velocities as you switch: water's 1,480 m/s already drags Z down, and air at ~330 m/s sits far lower still — hence the near-total reflection.

tasks.label: Push λ in steel below 1 mm — resolution air gives up
tasks.hint: You need about 6 MHz or more — far above the sub-1 MHz band that can cross an air gap, which is exactly the detail air coupling trades away.
